64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.

91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>

109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:

111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine

120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

168	## Rules

170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.

"It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).

The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.

204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
25	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
26	
27	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
28	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
29	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
30	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
31	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
32	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
33	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
34	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
35	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
36	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
37	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
38	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
39	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
40	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
41	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
42	
43	\begin{definition}
44	
45	\leanok
46	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
47	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
48	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
49	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
50	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
51	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
52	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
53	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
54	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
55	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
56	  charts. Suppose given:
57	  \begin{itemize}
58	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
59	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
60	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
61	      \[
62	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
63	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
64	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
65	      \]
66	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
67	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
68	  \end{itemize}
69	  subject to the \emph{module cocycle conditions} compatible with \(D\): the
70	  self-transition \(g_{ii}\) is the identity, and over each triple overlap the
71	  composite \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij}\) agrees with \(g_{ik}\) once transported
72	  through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\) (the multiplicative,
73	  \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition for the family
74	  \((g_{ij})\)). Out of this data the construction produces:
75	  \begin{enumerate}
76	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
77	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
78	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
79	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
80	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
81	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
82	  \end{enumerate}
83	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
84	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
85	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
86	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
87	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
88	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
89	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
90	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
91	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
92	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
93	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
94	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
95	
96	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
97	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
98	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
99	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
100	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
101	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
102	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
103	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
104	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
105	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
106	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
107	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
108	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
109	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
110	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
111	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
112	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
113	  built.
114	
115	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} There is no turn-key gluing of sheaves
116	  of modules over a scheme glue datum available off the shelf; the construction
117	  below is original to this development, and it is the most involved declaration of
118	  the chapter -- expect a multi-step build assembled from the primitives named here,
119	  not an appeal to a single ready-made result. Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
120	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
121	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
122	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
123	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
124	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
125	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
126	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). Restriction of a sheaf of modules
127	  to a chart is computed through the geometric restriction bridge of the
128	  development: for the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the
129	  pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) realises an equivalence between the sheaves of modules on
130	  \(X\) seen over the open \(U_i\) and the sheaves of modules on the open subscheme
131	  \(U_i\) itself -- the same open-immersion pullback restriction equivalence used to
132	  descend quasi-coherence along a basic open in the Quot-scheme construction. The
133	  transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a
134	  descent datum; gluing the local sections then yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with
135	  the restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the
136	  characterisation up to unique isomorphism rest on the locality of sections of a
137	  sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over the cover, agreeing on the overlaps,
138	  extends to a unique global section.
139	
140	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
141	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
142	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
143	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
144	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
145	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
146	\end{definition}
147	
148	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
149	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
150	
151	\begin{definition}
152	
153	\leanok
154	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
155	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
157	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
158	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
159	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
160	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
162	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
163	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
164	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
165	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
166	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
167	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
168	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
169	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
170	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
171	  \[
172	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
173	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
174	  \]
175	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
176	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
177	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
178	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
179	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
180	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
181	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
182	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
183	\end{definition}
184	
185	\begin{definition}
186	
187	\leanok
188	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
189	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
191	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
192	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
193	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
194	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
195	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
196	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
198	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
199	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
200	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
201	  %  by putting
202	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
203	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
204	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
205	  \[
206	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
207	  \]
208	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
209	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
210	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
211	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
212	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
213	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
214	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
215	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
216	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
217	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
218	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
219	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
220	\end{definition}
221	
222	\begin{definition}
223	
224	\leanok
225	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
226	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
228	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
229	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
230	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
231	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
232	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
233	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
234	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
235	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
236	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
237	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
238	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
239	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
240	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
241	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
242	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
243	  \[
244	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
245	  \]
246	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
247	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
248	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
249	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
250	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
251	  \[
252	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
253	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
254	  \]
255	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
256	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
257	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
258	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
259	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
260	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
261	\end{definition}
262	
263	\section{The functor of points}
264	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
265	
266	\begin{definition}
267	
268	\leanok
269	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
270	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
272	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
273	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
274	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
275	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
276	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
277	  % "represents the
278	  %  contravariant functor
279	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
280	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
281	  %  set of all equivalence classes
282	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
283	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
284	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
285	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
286	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
287	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
288	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
289	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
290	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
291	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
292	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
293	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
294	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
295	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
296	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
297	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
298	  \[
299	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
300	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
301	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
302	  \]
303	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
304	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
305	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
306	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
307	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
308	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
309	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
310	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
311	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\section{The universal property}
315	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
316	
317	\begin{theorem}
318	
319	\leanok
320	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
321	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
322	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
323	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
324	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
325	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
326	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
327	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
328	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
329	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
330	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
331	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
332	  %  contravariant functor
333	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
334	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
335	  %  set of all equivalence classes
336	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
337	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
338	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
339	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
341	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
342	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
343	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
344	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
345	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
346	  \[
347	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
348	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
349	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
350	    \qquad
351	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
352	      \;\longmapsto\;
353	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
354	  \]
355	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
356	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
357	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
358	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
359	  universal family.
360	\end{theorem}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
364	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
365	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
366	    def:grassmannian_functor}
367	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
368	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
369	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
370	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
371	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
372	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
373	
374	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
375	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
376	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
377	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
378	  the composite
379	  \[
380	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
381	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
382	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
383	  \]
384	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
385	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
386	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
387	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
388	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
389	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
390	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
391	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
392	
393	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
394	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
395	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
396	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
397	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
398	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
399	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
400	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
401	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
402	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
403	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
404	
405	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
406	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
407	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
408	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
409	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
410	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
411	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
412	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
413	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
414	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
415	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
416	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
417	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
418	
419	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
420	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
421	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
422	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
423	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
424	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
425	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
426	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
427	
428	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
429	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
430	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
431	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
432	\end{proof}

  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
  free sheaves'' primitive.

\begin{lemma}[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
  \[
    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
  \]
  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
\end{proof}
